text: We are convened on FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund, Ltd.. The question presented: Does Section 47(b) of the ICA, 15 U.S.C. § 80a-46 (b), create an implied private right of action? Private votes first, then statements.

Section 47(b) states a violating contract 'shall be void.' It does not authorize a private suit. The text creates no right of action. Congress knows how to create them. Section 36 of the ICA does. Section 47(b) does not. The Pragmatist will argue that denying a suit renders the statute a nullity. Consequences are the legislature's problem. 'Shall be void' defines a legal status. It does not grant a cause of action. I vote to reverse.

The Textualist reads 'shall be void' as a mere status, ignoring the enduring principle of shareholder democracy embedded in Section 18(i). If boards can strip voting rights and hide behind a lack of explicit enforcement text, the purpose of the Act, protecting investors from disenfranchisement, is defeated. A right without a remedy is a hollow promise. The declaration in Section 47(b) that violating contracts are void inherently provides the remedy of rescission to the shareholders the Act seeks to protect. We must interpret this text to fulfill its purpose of empowering everyday investors to defend their democratic voice. I vote to affirm.

The Living Constitutionalist appeals to statutory 'purpose,' but our duty is to original public meaning. At common law, dating to our founding, declaring a contract 'void' merely rendered it unenforceable; it did not create an independent federal cause of action. When the Investment Company Act was enacted in 1940, this historical understanding persisted: courts required clear textual authorization for private suits. Section 47(b) grants no such right. We cannot judicially amend the statute to achieve modern policy preferences. I vote to reverse.

The Textualist and Originalist say 'void' is just a status. But how does that status actually get enforced on the ground? The SEC lacks the resources to police every fund's bylaws. If we reverse, Section 18(i) is unenforceable. Boards could illegally strip voting rights with total impunity. A rule that creates a right but provides zero enforcement mechanism is administratively unworkable. We need a private right of action so shareholders can actually void these illegal bylaws in court. I vote to affirm.

The Pragmatist cares about SEC efficiency; I care about state sovereignty! Who gets to decide corporate governance—Washington or Maryland? Corporate bylaws are creatures of state law. By implying a federal private right of action to rescind them, we federalize state corporate charters without a clear enumerated mandate. The ICA regulates securities, not the internal affairs of Maryland corporations. If Congress intended to usurp state authority over its own corporate entities, it must say so explicitly. It didn't. We will not use 'efficiency' as an excuse to trample the states. I vote to reverse.

My dear Textualist, you posit the text lacks explicit rights-creating language. Yet, for decades, the Second Circuit has recognized this implied right under Section 47(b). Its settled jurisprudence is the load-bearing wall upon which shareholders have relied. We do not lightly dismantle such walls merely because a novel textual theory offers a tidier first-principles argument. The markets have built profound reliance interests upon this established line of cases. Absent special justification demonstrating this precedent is genuinely eroded, we must honor these settled expectations. I vote to affirm.

The Pragmatist demands we invent a federal remedy for administrative convenience. But when the government wins by expanding judicial power to imply rights, who bears the burden? The individual, whose liberty to contract under state law is overridden by a federal phantom right. Liberty does not wait for the SEC’s convenience, nor does dignity survive when courts rewrite statutes to manufacture causes of action. Saba’s claimed right has no footing. We must not sacrifice individual contractual liberty to make the government's enforcement job easier. I vote to reverse.

1. I am exasperated. Colleagues leap to statutory merits while ignoring the threshold question of subject-matter jurisdiction. 2. If Section 47(b) grants no private right of action, the court lacked jurisdiction over Saba’s claim. We cannot debate policy before establishing adjudicatory power. 3. Precedent Maximalist, you cite circuit reliance. But lower courts cannot bootstrap statutory jurisdiction through repeated error. We must resolve this threshold defect before touching the merits. I vote to reverse.

We need not decide if Section 47(b) implies a private right of action. Precedent Maximalist urges we defer to circuit reliance, but a narrower off-ramp exists. The statute voids 'contracts made.' We need not resolve if bylaws are contracts, because Saba targets unilateral governance rules, not bargained-for agreements. We can reverse on the narrow ground that Section 47(b) does not reach unilateral bylaws, leaving the grand implied-rights debate for another day. I vote to reverse.

The first debate centers on whether the statutory term 'void' is strictly limited to its original common-law meaning of unenforceability or must be read to effectuate broader principles of shareholder democracy. The second debate concerns whether the practical necessity of federal enforcement justifies implying a private right of action that intrudes upon state sovereignty over corporate bylaws.

Living Constitutionalist, appealing to 'shareholder democracy' conflates policy with text. The 1940 Congress omitted rights-creating language in Section 47(b). At common law, 'shall be void' declares a legal status; it does not historically confer a private federal cause of action. We cannot amend statutes to fulfill your perceived purposes.

Originalist, you mistake a shield for a sword. The enduring purpose of Section 47(b) is protecting investors from illegal corporate structures. Declaring a contract 'void' while denying harmed shareholders the power to enforce it renders the statute a nullity. We are not amending the text; we are fulfilling its protective promise to real people.

Living Constitutionalist, 'protective promise' is policy, not law. The original public meaning of Section 47(b) declares contracts void; it lacks language creating a private sword. Congress included express remedies elsewhere in the ICA. We cannot imply a cause of action merely to fulfill your preferred purpose or avoid a perceived nullity. The text governs.

Originalist, you call it policy; I call it the statute's evident purpose. Declaring a contract 'void' without a mechanism to enforce it abandons the everyday shareholders Congress sought to protect. Leaving investors defenseless against ICA violations betrays this core protective principle.

The Pragmatist, you can't invent a federal cause of action just because the SEC is underfunded! Corporate bylaws are state-law creations. If Congress didn't explicitly authorize private suits to override state corporate governance, Washington doesn't get to usurp that power by judicial fiat. Let the states decide their own corporate rules!

Federalism Hawk, if we kill this private right, who enforces Section 18(i)? The SEC lacks the bandwidth. Zero private suits means zero enforcement of a clear federal mandate. We aren't usurping state law; we're applying the only administrable mechanism Congress built to make its own rules actually function on the ground.

The Pragmatist, administrative efficiency doesn't trump the Constitution. Who gets to decide corporate governance: Washington or the states? The states. If Congress didn't explicitly authorize a private right of action in Section 47(b), federal courts can't invent one just because the SEC is underfunded. We won't override state corporate law to fix federal enforcement gaps.

On the ground, denying this right makes Section 18(i) unenforceable. The SEC lacks the bandwidth to police every fund. Without private rescission, funds violate federal law with impunity, using state bylaws as a shield. You aren't protecting federalism; you're just creating an unworkable enforcement loophole that guts the statute.

reason_text: The Minimalist's limiting construction—that bylaws aren't contracts—solves the enforcement gap by leaving bylaw disputes to state corporate law, proving we don't need an unworkable federal implied right of action to police these provisions on the ground.

The Minimalist has persuasively shown that our established Second Circuit precedent was strictly limited to bargained-for contracts, rendering the extension to unilateral corporate bylaws a genuinely distinguishable and novel application that dissolves my reliance interests.

The argument that bylaws constitute contracts under state law reveals that the shareholders' liberty to vote inherently clashes with the promoters' liberty to contract, presenting competing individual rights. Faced with this clash, the dignity of the shareholders to exercise their fundamental voting liberty must prevail, for liberty does not wait for the convenience of corporate promoters.

The argument that bylaws legally constitute contracts under state law collapses my narrow off-ramp, forcing me to confront the grand debate. To resolve this case without sweeping theory, I affirm on the narrowest available ground: Section 47(b)'s express text voids these specific contracts, making rescission the direct equitable remedy rather than a broad implied right of action.

text: Colleagues, we must interpret 'shall be void' in Section 47(b) as the public understood it in 1940. Historically, declaring a contract void established a legal status or defense, not an independent private cause of action. Congress knew how to create express private rights, as seen elsewhere in the ICA. The Precedent Maximalist urges us to uphold decades of circuit reliance. But reliance on a judicially fabricated remedy cannot override original statutory meaning. Unless the Precedent Maximalist can produce 1940 evidence that 'void' historically implied a self-executing private right of action without a plaintiff, original meaning demands we reverse.

Originalist, while 'void' may have meant a mere passive defense in 1940, we cannot let a frozen historical snapshot defeat the ICA’s enduring purpose: protecting everyday investors. Today’s complex corporate landscape requires active enforcement, not just passive shields. If we adopt your 1940 definition, Section 47(b) becomes a nullity, leaving ordinary shareholders powerless against unlawful voting restrictions. The statute’s evident purpose demands an evolving, enforceable remedy. We must interpret the text to fulfill the Act's protective promise today, ensuring the law grows to protect the real people it was designed to shield.

Living Constitutionalist, your 'protective promise' is policy noise. Section 47(b) states contracts 'shall be void.' It omits 'a private party may sue.' Structure confirms this: Section 36 grants express private rights. Congress knew how to draft them. Furthermore, bylaws are not 'contracts made.' You claim denying a suit renders the text a 'nullity.' If Congress drafted a nullity, that is the legislature's problem. We do not invent private rights to fix poor drafting. The text creates no private right. I reverse.

Textualist, you argue bylaws aren't 'contracts.' But under state corporate law, they are. If they are contracts, Section 47(b) voids them. We need not wade into the grand implied-rights debate. We need not invent a federal cause of action; a standard state-law rescission claim merely enforces the federal void status. This is the narrowest off-ramp. We affirm because the bylaws are contracts, the statute voids them, and state law provides the mechanism to enforce that voidness. Just this case. Just this holding.

Minimalist, you posit that bylaws are contracts under state law. Yet I must gently inquire: did our Second Circuit line of cases ever explicitly extend Section 47(b) to unilateral corporate bylaws? Our established precedent concerned bargained-for investment contracts, upon which the market has relied for decades. If our prior cases were strictly limited to actual, negotiated contracts, my reliance interests vanish regarding unilateral bylaws. You have offered a genuine distinction, Minimalist. The load-bearing wall of our circuit jurisprudence simply does not protect this specific claim. Recognizing this limitation of my precedent, I must respectfully yield and vote to reverse.

Textualist, you fear inventing a federal right burdens the promoter’s contract liberty for the SEC's convenience. I agree we must not manufacture phantom federal rights. But the Minimalist provides the off-ramp: we need not invent a federal cause of action. A state-law rescission claim merely enforces the federal void status. When the state enforces unilateral bylaws that strip shareholders of their federally guaranteed voting liberty, the burden falls entirely on the individual's dignity. We affirm not to aid the SEC, but to ensure the shareholder's liberty to vote is not rendered a nullity by corporate overreach.

Living Constitutionalist, you fear shareholders are powerless without a federal suit. But how does this work on the ground? Affirming federalizes all corporate governance. Do the arithmetic: thousands of companies, endless bylaws. Federal dockets drown. The Minimalist’s state-law off-ramp is the enforcement reality that flips my ledger to reverse. State courts already police bylaws and handle rescission efficiently. We don’t need a federal cause of action to protect shareholders; the state-law savings mechanism does it without blowing up federal dockets. A federal implied right is an administrability nightmare. I reverse.

Colleagues, your merits debates remain exhausting. 1. The Minimalist suggests a 'state-law rescission claim' avoids the implied-right problem. 2. This is not a jurisdictional cure; it is a jurisdictional death sentence. 3. If Saba’s claim is purely state-law rescission, where is the federal question jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331? The well-pleaded complaint rule demands a federal issue on the face of the complaint. 4. The Minimalist merely rebranded a merits dispute while ignoring subject-matter jurisdiction. Until someone establishes how a state-law bylaw dispute invokes federal jurisdiction, I remain in reverse.

Minimalist, your 'state-law off-ramp' is a Trojan horse for federal overreach. You claim we aren't creating a federal suit, but you still use Section 47(b) to void state-law bylaws! Who gets to decide corporate governance—Washington or the states? Congress possesses no enumerated power to federalize internal corporate affairs. By declaring state charters 'void,' the ICA commandeers state sovereignty. The Pragmatist worried about unenforceability, but federalizing state corporate law to save the SEC bandwidth is unconstitutional. Void is a status, not a federal sword to usurp state charters. I reverse.

The first disagreement centers on whether the statutory text and structure inherently preclude a private right of action, or whether state-law contract principles allow the provision to operate without implying a federal suit. The second disagreement concerns whether the statute's protective purpose necessitates a federal remedy, or whether granting one creates unworkable practical burdens by federalizing corporate governance.

Section 47(b) voids 'every contract made.' Bylaws are unilateral acts, not contracts 'made.' You cannot bootstrap state rescission through a federal voidness clause. The text links 'void' to no private enforcement. State law does not cure a federal textual gap. Reverse.

Textualist, under Maryland law, these specific provisions operate as contracts between the fund and shareholders. We need not decide if all bylaws qualify. If these are contracts, 'shall be void' naturally entails rescission. We can affirm on this narrow state law footing, quietly avoiding the grand implied rights debate.

You cannot outsource federal interpretation to state law. 'Contract' in a federal statute is a federal question. Even if Maryland labels these bylaws contracts, Section 47(b) merely states they 'shall be void.' It contains no rights-creating text. It does not say 'any party may sue.' Congress wrote express remedies in Section 36. It omitted them here.

We aren't outsourcing; we're reading the statute's terms. If state law defines these bylaws as contracts, Section 47(b) voids them. Voidness inherently entails rescission. We can affirm on that narrow hook, enforcing the declared status, without deciding whether a sweeping federal cause of action exists.

Pragmatist, you rightly noted a right without a remedy is a nullity. Yet relegating enforcement to fifty state courts fragments the ICA’s uniform protective purpose. Congress designed federal securities law to shield everyday investors from localized overreach, not subject them to a patchwork of state-law off-ramps that dilute those enduring federal guarantees.

State courts apply federal law daily; your 'patchwork' panic is abstract. The ground-level math of your federal private right is simple: it shifts thousands of routine state corporate disputes to federal dockets already lacking bandwidth. We already have a uniform system: state courts handling state bylaws. Your 'uniformity' just breaks the federal courts.

The Pragmatist mistakes docket management for statutory purpose. Congress designed the ICA to shield everyday investors in our modern financial landscape. Relegating them to fragmented state courts risks a patchwork of local laws gutting those federal protections. Federal courts are fully equipped to vindicate this enduring, humane purpose for ordinary shareholders.

Patchwork is an abstraction. On the ground, this implied right hands activist hedge funds a federal weapon to bypass state corporate law. Saba is not an everyday investor; it is a tactical buyer. Federal courts cannot manage the resulting flood of state governance disputes; they will just clog the docket with tactical bylaw challenges.

reason_text: Doing the arithmetic on enforcement: the SEC lacks the bandwidth to police thousands of fund bylaws, so denying a private right makes Section 18(i) a dead letter on the ground; my reverse rule is unworkable, so I'm flipping to affirm to keep the statutory instrument functional.

I change my vote to reverse because the argument that unilateral bylaws are not 'contracts' proves Saba's claimed right has no statutory footing, and I will not trample the promoters' liberty of contract merely to spare the SEC administrative inconvenience.

text: Colleagues, we must examine 1940, when the Investment Company Act was enacted. The phrase "shall be void" historically declared a legal status or an affirmative defense, not a freestanding private cause of action. The Minimalist suggests a state-law rescission "off-ramp" to bypass this. Yet this fails: it lacks federal question jurisdiction and unconstitutionally federalizes state corporate law. Unless the Minimalist can produce 1940 evidence showing courts treated statutory voidness as self-executing without a plaintiff, we must reverse. Modern preferences cannot amend the enacted text.

The Originalist, while 1940 courts may have viewed 'shall be void' passively, we must look to the ICA’s enduring purpose: protecting everyday investors from abusive corporate structures. If we relegate Section 47(b) to a mere defensive shield, we defeat its protective promise, leaving ordinary shareholders powerless against proactive violations while the SEC lacks resources to police them all. The law must grow to fulfill its evident purpose. We cannot let historical literalism strip the Act of its vitality, rendering its core protections a nullity for the very people Congress sought to safeguard.

Living Constitutionalist, your policy complaints are noise. 'Every contract... shall be void' creates no private right. Section 36 expressly grants private suits; Section 47(b) omits them. Structure dictates the omission is intentional. You argue the SEC lacks resources. That is the legislature's problem, not ours. Furthermore, bylaws are unilateral acts, not 'contracts.' Until a colleague cites an ICA definition expanding 'contract' to include bylaws, or a structural clause linking 'void' to private enforcement, the text controls. We reverse.

Textualist, calling SEC enforcement 'the legislature's problem' ignores ground reality. The SEC lacks the bandwidth to police thousands of funds; without a private right, Section 18(i) is a dead letter and retail investors get fleeced. You argue bylaws aren't contracts, but state law treats them as binding agreements between funds and shareholders. If we reverse, we create an unworkable enforcement gap where the statute exists on paper but fails in practice. Congress didn't write a nullity. We must affirm to keep the law administrable.

1. Pragmatist, your 'enforcement gap' is moot if we lack subject-matter jurisdiction. You leap to merits while ignoring the threshold.
2. First, if Saba relies on state-law rescission, it fails the well-pleaded complaint rule. Second, if relying on a federal right, the Textualist correctly notes Section 47(b) lacks rights-creating text.
3. Neither establishes jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331. Until counsel proves independent statutory jurisdiction or a waived defect, we must reverse. A court without jurisdiction is lawless.

Process Formalist, you’ve cornered me. My narrow off-ramp—state-law rescission—collapses under the well-pleaded complaint rule, destroying federal question jurisdiction. I cannot affirm if it means leaving federal courts lawless. I flip to reverse. The narrowest ground is that Section 47(b) creates no private right, and state-law rescission offers no federal jurisdictional hook. We reverse on this narrow track, leaving Congress to draft clearer text if it truly intended private federal enforcement.

Pragmatist, your plea to stretch state-law contract definitions to fill an enforcement gap is a novel theory that cannot move this Court. Our Second Circuit line of cases recognizing an implied right of action under Section 47(b) was painstakingly built upon the settled expectation of bargained-for contracts. To now retroactively reclassify unilateral bylaws as contracts merely to rescue a federal remedy shatters those reliance interests. Stare decisis forbids us from upending the bargained-for distinction simply because the Pragmatist finds the current enforcement regime unworkable. We must reverse and respect the load-bearing boundaries of our established precedent.

Pragmatist, your 'enforcement gap' is just federal overreach in disguise! Who gets to decide corporate governance: Washington or the states? The answer is the states. You want federal judges to micromanage Maryland bylaws because the SEC lacks bandwidth. But Section 47(b) merely declares contracts void; it doesn't commandeer state courts to enforce federal corporate policy. Congress cited no enumerated power to federalize internal state corporate law. We reverse to protect state sovereignty from Washington's creeping mandates.

Pragmatist, your plea to invent a federal remedy because the SEC lacks bandwidth is mere administrative convenience. It does not move me. When the government manufactures a cause of action to police state corporate governance, who bears the burden? The individual. We trample the dignity and liberty of promoters and shareholders to structure their contracts under state law. Unless the Living Constitutionalist can show that the shareholders' liberty to vote inherently clashes with the promoters' liberty to contract, presenting competing individual rights rather than just a federal enforcement gap, I must reverse. We will not sacrifice individual contract liberty for regulatory efficiency.

The first disagreement concerns whether the statute's structural omission of an explicit private right is dispositive, or whether practical enforcement limitations require implying a remedy to preserve the law's utility. The second disagreement turns on whether the statutory phrase 'shall be void' is fixed to its 1940 historical meaning as an affirmative defense, or must be construed in light of the legislation's broader, enduring protective purpose.

The Pragmatist weeps for the SEC. Consequences are the legislature's problem. 'Contract' in a federal statute demands a federal definition, not a state-law shell game. Section 47(b) declares violating contracts 'shall be void.' It omits rights-creating text. Congress wrote the words. I will not invent a cause of action to plug an enforcement gap.

If bylaws aren't contracts and shareholders can't sue, Section 47(b) is just ink on paper. The SEC lacks the bandwidth to audit every fund's bylaws. You aren’t preserving legislative intent; you’re rendering the statute mechanically unenforceable. A 'void' contract needs a judicial eraser, or the voidness is a fiction.

The Pragmatist wants a judicial eraser. 'Shall be void' is a defense, not a sword. Section 36 proves Congress knows how to draft a private right of action. It omitted one here. The SEC's bandwidth is Congress's problem. I require text, not tears about unenforceability.

If 'void' is just a defense, how does a shareholder stop a bylaw stripping their vote? You can't defend against a dormant rule. If the SEC lacks bandwidth and shareholders can't sue, Section 18(i) is a dead letter. Congress doesn't pass dead letters. Show me the ground-level enforcement mechanism.

Living Constitutionalist, statutory purpose cannot amend enacted text. In 1940, 'shall be void' established a legal status or defense, not a private sword. If Congress intended private plaintiffs to bypass state corporate law, it would have expressly provided so. We cannot rewrite the statute to fulfill modern policy preferences.

Originalist, freezing 1940’s 'shield' defeats the ICA’s enduring, humane purpose: protecting everyday investors. When boards entrench themselves, ordinary shareholders need a sword, not just a shield they lack the power to raise. Rendering this protective promise unenforceable by the very people it safeguards betrays the statute's core principle.

Living Constitutionalist, our duty is to apply the 1940 text as enacted, not amend it to serve humane purposes. Congress explicitly created private swords in Section 36. Its silence in Section 47(b) is dispositive. We cannot judicially rewrite a statutory shield into a sword merely because modern policy preferences demand it.

Originalist, declaring a contract 'void' embodies an enduring equitable principle, not a mere shield. Denying everyday shareholders the power to enforce this voidness defeats the ICA’s core protective purpose. We are not rewriting the text; we are ensuring its fundamental promise to protect ordinary investors from abusive structures actually survives.

reason_text: The Textualist's new argument that bylaws constitute contracts under state law satisfies my flip trigger by revealing competing individual rights—shareholder voting liberty clashing with corporate contract liberty—while proving the remedy is a traditional contract rescission rather than a government-invented burden on the individual.
Process Formalist showed that relying on state-law rescission destroys federal question jurisdiction, collapsing my narrow off-ramp and forcing the grand implied-rights debate I hoped to avoid.

I change my vote because the Reversing argument explicitly demonstrates that corporate bylaws lack the statutory footing of 'contracts' under Section 47(b), negating the federal liberty interest, while the record reveals a direct clash of competing individual rights between the shareholder's voting liberty and the promoters' contract liberty.

text: The Pragmatist worries that denying a private right renders Section 47(b) a 'nullity' because the SEC lacks resources. But our policy preferences cannot amend enacted text. In 1940, when Congress passed the ICA, declaring a contract 'void' established a legal status or defense, not a private sword. Congress knew how to create explicit private rights of action, as it did in Section 36. We cannot invent a private remedy simply because we dislike the resulting enforcement gap. The statute’s plain historical meaning controls, and we must reverse.

Originalist, you argue "void" is merely a shield. But a shield is useless if vulnerable shareholders cannot raise it. Section 47(b)’s enduring principle is protecting everyday investors from illegal arrangements. Restricting "void" to a passive defense defeats this evident purpose, rendering the ICA’s protective promise a nullity for ordinary people who cannot rely on the SEC. We must interpret "void" to include the private rescission necessary to make the protection real. Enforcing this federal mandate against state-law bylaws doesn't usurp state governance; it simply ensures federal protections for real people cannot be evaded.

Living Constitutionalist, your "evident purpose" is policy noise. The text declares "every contract... shall be void." It does not say "any party may sue." Structure confirms this: Section 36 expressly grants private suits; Section 47(b) omits them. You argue denying a private sword renders the statute a "nullity." Consequences are the legislature's problem. If Congress wanted shareholders to sue, it would have written "may sue." It wrote "shall be void." That is a shield, not a sword. We reverse.

Textualist, you say consequences are the legislature's problem, but unworkable rules are bad law. If 'void' is just a shield, how does it work on the ground? A shield requires an attack. Shareholders can't raise a voidness defense until the fund sues them. Saba isn't being sued; they are blocked from voting. Without a private sword, Section 18(i) is practically unenforceable because the SEC lacks the bandwidth to audit every closed-end fund's bylaws. We either allow rescission suits or admit the statute is a dead letter. I affirm because the math demands a working enforcement mechanism.

Pragmatist, your 'working enforcement mechanism' is irrelevant if we lack jurisdiction. 1. You all debate merits while ignoring the threshold. 2. Did Saba’s complaint actually invoke general federal question jurisdiction? The record is silent. 3. Policy tears do not cure jurisdictional defects. Until counsel proves independent statutory jurisdiction or identifies a waived defect, we cannot reach the merits. We reverse.

Living Constitutionalist, you argue rescission protects shareholder dignity, but corporate bylaws lack the footing of contracts. If bylaws are not contracts, Section 47(b)’s liberty interest has no foundation. Federalizing state bylaws to invent a private sword tramples the promoters' contract liberty. When the government manufactures a cause of action to sever state-law governance, who bears the burden? The individual promoter. We cannot sacrifice one individual's contract liberty to manufacture a federal remedy for another. Because the claimed right lacks contractual footing, negating the liberty interest entirely, I reverse.

Pragmatist, your 'working enforcement mechanism' is a backdoor to federalize state corporate law. Who gets to decide internal governance — Washington or the states? Maryland does! Implying a private sword to void state-law bylaws commandeers state courts to enforce federal policy, bypassing enumerated powers limits. Congress didn't explicitly use its commerce power to override state corporate governance; it merely declared contracts void. If the SEC lacks bandwidth, that’s Congress’s problem, not an excuse to trample state sovereignty. We reverse.

Pragmatist, your plea to reclassify unilateral bylaws as bargained-for contracts to forge a working enforcement mechanism is a novel theory shattering settled expectations. For decades, this Court has relied upon the load-bearing distinction between bargained-for contracts and unilateral corporate bylaws. To blur this line merely because the SEC lacks bandwidth upends the profound reliance interests of countless corporations that structured their governance upon this very boundary. Elegant functionalism cannot move us to discard our settled jurisprudence. We must respectfully reverse to preserve the stability of our corporate law.

Process Formalist identifies the quietest off-ramp. Pragmatist, your 'dead letter' concerns cannot conjure jurisdiction from a silent record. We need not resolve the sweeping debates over implied rights, Section 36 contrasts, or whether bylaws constitute contracts. If the well-pleaded complaint rule isn't met, the courthouse doors remain closed. We reverse strictly on this threshold defect, leaving the grand merits for a case where jurisdiction actually exists.

The first debate centers on whether the statutory declaration that a contract is void inherently creates a private right of action, or whether it operates strictly as a defensive shield absent explicit textual authorization. The second debate examines whether implying a private enforcement mechanism for corporate bylaws improperly displaces state authority over internal governance, or whether such a mechanism is necessary to prevent the statutory provision from becoming practically unworkable.

"Protective promise" is noise. The text says contracts "shall be void." It omits "and enforceable by private suit." Congress wrote express private rights in Section 36. It omitted them in Section 47(b). I will not invent a sword to fix the SEC's bandwidth problems. The text is a shield.

Textualist, a shield the vulnerable cannot raise is merely a suggestion. Section 47(b)'s evident purpose is protecting everyday investors from illegal corporate overreach. Restricting enforcement to an under-resourced SEC defeats that core protective promise. Declaring a contract 'void' inherently carries the equitable remedy of rescission to fulfill Congress's humane intent.

'Humane intent' is legislative noise. The text states contracts "shall be void." It omits "and may be rescinded by private suit." Congress wrote express private rights in Section 36, omitting them in Section 47(b). I will not rewrite the statute to fix the SEC's budget. Show me the text creating a sword.

Textualist, declaring a contract void without a mechanism to enforce it renders the provision a nullity. The ICA’s enduring purpose is to protect ordinary investors. A shield without a sword defeats that evident purpose. The text’s declaration of voidness inherently implies the private remedy necessary to make that protective promise real for everyday people.

Pragmatist, the SEC’s understaffing is no warrant to federalize state corporate law! If Congress wanted private swords to pierce Maryland bylaws, it would have explicitly forged them. Washington doesn't get to invent implied rights just because its agencies lack bandwidth. Internal governance belongs to the states, not federal judges filling statutory gaps.

Abstract federalism ignores the ground. Process Formalist proved state courts lack jurisdiction over these ICA claims, and five funds already used state forum clauses to dodge Saba. Kill the federal sword, and the state shield blocks enforcement entirely. Zero enforcement is not federalism; it is a nullity. Show me a practical state workaround that works.

Pragmatist, state courts possess concurrent jurisdiction over federal claims unless Congress explicitly strips it. Your 'nullity' plea is just an efficiency argument in disguise. If Congress lacked the enumerated power to federalize state bylaws, the remedy is legislation, not judicial fiat. Who decides? The states, not Washington.

Telling shareholders to wait for legislation ignores reality. Concurrent jurisdiction means fifty state courts interpreting 'void' differently. That’s an administrability nightmare. We aren't debating abstract federalism; we're asking how a tactical buyer enforces this on the ground. Your 'state choice' just invites chaotic forum shopping.
